coredata.dc:title: One step hydrothermal functionalization of gold nanoparticles with folic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Vostrikova, Anna M.
Kokorina, Alina A.
Mitrophanova, Anastassia N.
Sindeeva, Olga A.
Sapelkin, Andrei V.
Sukhorukov, Gleb B.
Goryacheva, Irina Y.
coredata.dc:description: A new fundamental concept for one-step in-situ functionalization of gold nanoparticles (GNPs) with folic acid using hydrothermal treatment is described. Hydrothermal treatment has been tuned to increase the light emission from the pterin moiety of folic acid molecule, while retain its structure and functionality, thus providing a simple route to multimodal tags for a variety of in vitro and in vivo biomedical applications. Successful functionalization of GNPs with the biological ligand is confirmed by specific binding with anti-folic acid antibody.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Folic acid
Biological ligand
Hydrothermal treatment
Functionalization

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mouse anti-FA antibodies binding with GNPs
Specific binding of F-GNP with Anti-FA antibody

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structure and characteristic parts of the folic acid.
Scheme of gold nanoparticles synthesis, functionalization and interaction with antibody.

Effect of the hydrothermal treatment time on the absorbance (black) and emission (red) spectra of hydrothermally treated folic acid solution (C = 1*10−3 M), diluted 10 times (A). Normalized emission spectra (B).
Infra-red (A) and Raman data (B) for the FA, FA-HT and F-GNP.

Absorbance spectra of initial GNPs (purple, solid), GNPs after hydrothermal treatment (purple, dashed), mixture of FA and GNPs before hydrothermal treatment (black), GNPs after hydrothermal treatment in the presence of FA, stock (blue, solid) and 10 times diluted (blue, dashed) (A). Emission spectra of hydrothermally treated GNPs in the absence (B) and in the presence of folic acid, F-CNDs (10 times diluted) (C).
Schematic diagram of F-GNPs binding to mouse anti-FA antibodies (A) non-specific mouse IgG (B), BSA (C) and empty well (D). Absorbance spectra of corresponding wells (E).
Schematic diagram of F-GNPs (A), hydrothermally treated GNPs (B) and GNPs (C) binding to mouse anti-FA antibodies (D). Absorbance spectra of corresponding wells (E).

Thus, these experiments demonstrate that HT treatment of GNPs with FA results in a coating of GNPs with the modified FA. This modified FA shows increased PL while retaining specific binding to anti-FA antibody. The latter is most likely due to preserved structure of the pterin moiety.

head.ce:title: One step hydrothermal functionalization of gold nanoparticles with folic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Saratov State University, Astrakhanskaya, 83, Saratov, 410012, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Saratov State University, Astrakhanskaya, 83, Saratov, 410012, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Queen Mary University of London End Road, London, E1 4NS, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Queen Mary University of London End Road

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Queen Mary University of London End Road, London, E1 4NS, U.K.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fundamental concept for one-step in-situ functionalization of gold nanoparticles (GNPs) with folic acid using hydrothermal treatment is described. Hydrothermal treatment has been tuned to increase the light emission from the pterin moiety of folic acid molecule, while retain its structure and functionality, thus providing a simple route to multimodal tags for a variety of in vitro and in vivo biomedical applications. Successful functionalization of GNPs with the biological ligand is confirmed by specific binding with anti-folic acid antibody.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (pteroyl-L-glutamic acid, FA) or folate is an essential compound for live biochemistry, it is involved in the process of synthesis of DNA and RNA and in the metabolism of amino acids during cell division. FA shows no toxicity (negative effects), is fully involved in biochemical cycles and is processed in a wide range of intracellular processes and can act as a ligand for folate receptors on cell surface. As a consequence, there is a significant interest in surface modification of nanoparticles of different nature with FA for the purposes of targeted delivery via folate receptors. Particular interest in addressing folate receptors is related to cell targeting, as the surface of a large variety of cancer cells is commonly overexpress folate receptors, while it is relatively weakly expressed in normal healthy cells [1].

Gold nanoparticles (GNP) found a very wide application in medical research, analysis and catalysis [2–5]. FA capped GNPs and GNP-based nanostructures are used for tumor targeting, imaging, and site-specific therapy [6–8] making research in GNPs modification with FA a highly active filed in nanoparticles functionalization. It is now well-know [9] that it is pterin end of the FA molecule (Fig. 1 ) that binds by folate receptors.

The most common approach for FA conjugation with GNPs is surface capping with molecules or layers that contain active groups, following with covalent binding of FA [10,11]. However, current methods are elaborate, multistep and time-consuming, require additional chemical reagents for surface functionalization that could cause toxic effects. Hence, development of a protocol which avoids extra chemicals and simplify the methodology should underpin the bio-friendly use of modified nanoparticles.

Furthermore, it would be highly desirable if F-GNP system can be efficiently traced in-vitro (or indeed in-vivo) to verify and provide an effective quantitative measure of targeted delivery. In that context it is well-known that FA exhibits weak (quantum yield (QY) is less than 0.005 [12,13] for acid and base forms of FA, 0.00045 for neutral form [14]) fluorescence originating from the pterin moiety. The low QY is due to the electron transfer to the p-aminobenzoic acid moiety followed by non-radiative energy decay [15–17]. Thus, if such electron transfer can be precluded – for example, by removing or modifying the p-aminobenzoic acid moiety – one would expect increase in fluorescence emission. Here we show that by carefully tuning hydrothermal treatment (HT) time and temperature we can accomplish this objective.

The aim of this work is to develop just such a novel way of GNP modification which involves the in-situ formation of FA-derived structures on GNP surface. To this end, we conducted HT of FA to optimize the synthesis time. We then applied this methodology to GNPs with FA (F-GNPs). The hydrothermal way of functionalization was chosen because of absence of any additional reagents in this one-step procedure, which can also be scaled up relatively easily. Finally, presence of characteristic folate structures on the surface of F-GNPs and maintenance of biological ligand properties was verified by binding with specific to FA antibody. The scheme of the processes is presented in Fig. 2 .

FA (Reagent and materials section as well as Equipment list see in Supporting Information) was modified by HT in bidistilled water. The typical procedure includes preparation of FA water suspension (1000 ml), using 0.44 g (or 0.0018 g) of FA, allowing to obtain solution 1*10−3 M (or 4*10-6 M). A portion of this suspension (3 ml) was then transferred into a glass cup, placed into the Teflon cup with tight-fitting cover, put inside stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for a certain selected time. The resulting solution was cooled at room temperature and centrifuged (20 min, 7500 g). After centrifugation solutions were stored at 4 °C.

F-GNPs were synthesized by capping of GNPs with FA using hydrothermal synthesis under conditions developed in the previous step. The typical procedure includes mixing of 2 ml of GNPs solution and 2 ml of 1*10−3 M FA water suspension. Consequently, GNPs concentration in F-GNPs is two times less than in the initial GNP solution. The mixture was then transferred into a glass cup, placed into the Teflon cup with tight-fitting cover, put inside stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for an hour. The resulting solution was cooled at room temperature and the uplayer solution contained product was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. The filtrate was centrifuged (20 min, 7500 g) and sediment was dissolved in the same amount of water and used for further experiments. The temporal stability of F-GNPs at room temperature was tested by measuring the absorbance spectra after sample preparation over a period of a few days.

F-GNPs binding with proteins. First, 50 μL of mouse anti-FA antibodies (IgG), non-specific mouse IgG, or BSA (1 μg/ml in 0.1 M CBB, рH 9.6) were added to the microplate wells and kept overnight. The washing was performed in triplicate with 300 μL of 0.05 M PBS, рH 7.4. After 100 μL of F-GNP solution contained 0.5% BSA were added to each well and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, then removed. Before the recording of the absorption spectra the washing step with 300 μL of 0.05 M PBS, рH 7.4 was performed. The absorbance spectra were recorded using a microplate spectrophotometer. All determinations were made at least in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mouse anti-FA antibodies binding with GNPs
ce:section.ce:para: First, 50 μL of mouse anti-FA antibodies (IgG), (1 μg/ml in 0.1 M CBB, рH 9.6) were added to the microplate wells and kept overnight. The washing was performed triplicate with 300 μL of 0.05 M PBS, рH 7.4. After 100 μL of F-GNP, GNP or hydrothermally treated GNP solutions (all contained 0.5% BSA) were added to wells and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C, then removed. Before the recording of the absorbance spectra the washing step with 300 μL of 0.05 M PBS, рH 7.4 was performed. The absorbance spectra were recorded using a microplate spectrophotometer. All determinations were made at least in triplicate.

The motivation for this work was development and testing of a new concept of expanding the HT of a carbon precursor to functionalization of GNPs while preserving the inherent properties of the precursor molecule. HT of carbon precursors are widely described for the synthesis of photoluminescent CNDs [18–20]. As a precursor, FA was chosen because of the high biological impact, the importance of its properties as a biological ligand in cancer visualization and theranostics research and, consequently, the need for simple non-toxic approaches for modification of various structures with FA. Here, we lowered synthesis temperature down to 200 °C and tuned the time to preserve the pterin moiety of FA molecule.

Because of low FA solubility (4*10−6 M at neutral pH), the higher FA concentrations results in suspension formation in water. For this reason, we compared FA solutions at two different concentrations: 4*10−6 M (this concentration was chosen to ensure folate is fully in ionic form) and 1*10-3 M (such suspension is visually turbid and become transparent after HT) to have enough carbon material available to obtain suitable concentration of folate-based nanostructures. FA-HT spectra obtained at two initial concentrations show no significant difference in the profiles of the absorption, excitation and emission peaks (see Figure S1). This gives us a reason to conclude that under HT conditions folate excess above the solubility concentration does not have a significant effect on the spectral properties of FA-HT, hence 1*10-3 M FA was used for all further experiments.

Effect of HT time (15, 30, 60 and 120 min; 200 °C) on the absorbance and emission was investigated since our main goal was to keep intact the characteristic FA fragments and properties of biological ligand, not just to obtain bright fluorescent structures. Stability of typical pterin absorbance (persistent bands at 282 and 350 nm related to high- and low- energy bands in pterin, respectively [21–23]) was used as an indicator that FA retains its properties as a ligand following HT process. The corresponding emission and absorbance spectra are shown in Fig. 3 . One can see from the absorbance spectra (Fig. 3A) that the pterin absorbance bands are stable up to 60 min. At the same time, photoluminescence (PL) shows significant increase between 30 min and 60 min of HT treatment. It is well-known [21–23] that emission at around 450 nm originates from the pterin moiety of FA. The normalized PL data (Fig. 3B) clearly demonstrate that while PL intensity is increasing, the spectral shape does not change, suggesting that PL does originate from pterin moiety even at 120 min synthesis time. Thus, 60 min treatment time has been chosen for subsequent experiments based on the optical spectroscopy measuremets. It was also found that relative QY grows from 0.2% at 15 min. to 3% at 60 min. It is very important to stress that the shape and position of the emission spectra remain the same as can be clearly seen from the normalized spectra (Fig. 3B), confirming the stability of initial FA luminophore. TEM image is presented on Figure S2A. Furthermore, gel-electrophoretic separation (Figure S2B) of FA before and after treatment shows that bands corresponding to FA and FA-HT are located the same distance, ingicating similar mobility.

We also carried out infra-red absorption and Raman measurments of FA and FA-HT to gain further understanding of the effect of HT treatment. IR data (Fig. 4 A) suggest that no significant changes to the molecular structure takes place as a result of HT treatment of FA. This is consistent with the absorption and gel-electrophoresis data. The only features of note are two relatively weak, but prominent sharp peaks appearing in the range of 1400-1550 cm−1 (marked with arrwos in Fig. 4A), which are most likely related to the aromatic CC bonds [24]. A decrease in the intensity of the band at around 3450 cm-1 may point to a reduction in the hydrogen-bonded groups (i.e. OH) and is consistent with an increase in PL intensity since these groups are most abandoned at the p-aminobenzoic of the FA molecule responsible for PL quenching.

Raman data (Fig. 4B) are much more revealing (all the modes indicated by arrow have been labeled according to [25,26]). One can see a significant evolution of all major features as a result of HT treatment. Most notable is significant decrease in the strength of the signal from OH, C–H and CH2 modes. The latter in particular can only be present in the p-aminobenzoic acid bridge. At the same time, the N-H modes that can be associated with the pterin moiety can still be observed despite significant decrease in the signal intensity.

Overall, we can conclude that since the p-aminobenzoic acid bridge in FA acts as an internal PL quencher that efficiently deactivates the excited singlet state of the pterin moiety [15], the observed increase in the emission intensity can be tentatively attributed to disruption of energy transfer to the p-aminobenzoic acid moiety.

Absorbance spectra of GNP before and after HT, as well as F-GNP, are presented in Fig. 5
A. The position of the surface plasmon maximum of GNPs is practically unchanged: 522 and 523 before and after HT; 525 nm for F-GNPs.

One can see from the absorbance spectra of diluted FA-HT that it contains typical FA (and pterin) absorption band (λmax =282 nm). Following HT of GNPs a weak PL emission is observed (Fig. 5B), while in the presence of FA the emission intensity is significantly higher (Fig. 5C). The relative QY of F-GNPs is low (1%, less than for FA-HT (3%), but higher than for FA (0.2%)).

Emission intensity of F-GNPs solution (Fig. 5C) is obviously lower than for FA-HT (Fig. 3A), even taking into account FA dilution by a factor of 2 for F-GNP preparation. To check possible quenching of folate residues emission by GNPs via energy transfer, emission spectra of F-GNP were compared with emission spectra of the mixture of equal volumes of FA-HT and GNPs. This mixture has the same initial concentrations of FA and GNPs as F-GNPs, but no close proximity between folate residues and GNPs as in F-GNPs. The same spectral shape and intensity of the emission peaks indicates no significant effects of energy transfer from hydrothermally treated folate structures on the GNP’ surface to GNPs. Thus, we conclude that a decrease in emission intensity for F-GNP compared with FA-HT is simply a result of photon absorption by GNPs.

IR spectrum of F-GNP (Fig. 4A) shows no new signals compared to FA-HT. Comparison of FA-HT and F-GNP Raman spectra (Fig. 4B) suggests that FA-HT and F-GNP carbon-based systems are of similar structure. According to the TEM image, the FA layer on the surface of GNP is very thin, probably presented by only one layer of the pterin residues (Figure S3). This is confirmed by a very small change of the average hydrodynamic diameter of F-GNPs (38 nm) compared to the value of hydrothermally treated GNPs (36 nm) (Table S1). It is important to mention dramatic change in zeta-potential: −46 mV for F-GNPs comparing with −10 mV for GNPs treated without FA (Table S1, Figure S4); this change confirms successful modification of GNPs surface.

To examine F-GNPs colloidal stability, a 100 μl aliquot of GNP solution was transferred to a microplate well and mixed with 100 μl of water or 1 M NaCl, leading to a solution with a final concentration of ∼0.5 M NaCl. No color change associated with aggregation was observed for F-GNPs, while for GNPs before and after HT aggregation in the presence of NaCl was clearly seen (Figure S5, non-stabilized GNPs aggregate in the presence of salt solution resulting in color change). These experiments distinctly show the excellent colloidal stability of F-GNPs. Increasing of stability via hydrothermal treatment could indicate formation of stable bound between surface of GNP and nitrogen atoms of pterin moiety. Ability of charge − neutral amine/gold surface interaction described by a weak covalent bond [27] and used to stabilize GNPs [28–30]. The long-term stability study shows the decreasing of the GNP plasmonic band for ˜ 15% in 6 month and for ˜ 25% in 9–12 months (Figure S6).

Using calculated values of GNP concentration (7*1011 nanoparticles per ml) and calculated percentage of FA binding to GNPs (45%) the amount of FA molecules per each GNP was calculated as 1.8*105 molecules. For 20 nm sphere surface area is 1.2*104 nm2, so per each FA molecular calculated GNP surface area is 0.07 nm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific binding of F-GNP with Anti-FA antibody
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the hypotheis that pterin moiety suffers no significant damage during HT treatment, while PL is increased, we carried out two sets of experiments based on F-GNP interaction with anti-FA antibody. To monitor F-GNPs interaction with anti-FA antibodies GNP surface plasmon absorption band was used and it was found that in the presence of BSA (0.5%) this band shifts by 10 nm to the longer wavelength (to 535 nm, Figure S7) as a result of the changes in the dielectric nature surrounding the GNPs due to BSA presence.

For control of binding anti-FA antibody at dilutions 1/100, 1/200 and 1/400 (related to 2.8*1013, 1.4*1013 and 0.7*1013 antibodies per well) were used for control of F-GNPs binding (4*1010 nanoparticles per well, according to dilutions). On the base of the ratio of the plasmonic peak intensities of F-GNPs bound to the well (Figure S8) to F-GNP solution in the well, the amount of bound F-GNPs was calculated as 1.5*1010, 1.1*1010 and 0.6*1010 nanoparticles per well for anti-FA antibody dilutions 1/100, 1/200 and 1/400, respectively.

We show that HT treatment of FA molecule results in an increase of PL QY by a factor of 15 and 5 for FA-HTs and F-GNPs, respectively. We attribute this increase in QY to the modifications of the part (p-aminobenzoic acid) of the molecule responsible for PL quenching in pure FA. Furthermore, HT treatment of FA by the method described here results in aggregation of the synthesis products into small nanoparticles. We used this tendency for aggregation together with increased PL for stabilization and functionalization of GNPs to obtain F-GNPs. We found that thus produced F-GNPs retained properties of precursor molecules as biological ligand and show specific binding to anti-FA antibody. Thus, we show that hydrothermal stabilization provides a straightforward and facile way to functionalize GNPs, thus producing a hybrid system capable of specific binding while showing increased level of PL. The stability of pterin moiety in FA molecule via HT and its role in GNPs stabilization was shown. This method can be further expanded for stabilization and functionalization of various other nanoparticles with folate, for example, mesoporous silica nanoparticles [31], silica modified up-conversion nanoparticles [32], graphene oxide nanosheets [33], carbon nanotubes [34] or quantum dots [35,36] and other which can undergo the hydrothermal process. Our approach demonstrates the functionalization of nanoparticles using HT with carbon material could be a promising way to obtain multimodal tags for a variety of in vitro and in vivo biomedical applications as well as for immunoassay.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Russian Science Foundation (Project 16-13-10195).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2019.05.072.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
